It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Partisan politics, welfare states, and environmental policy outputs in the OECD countries, 1975–2005 Sijeong Lim* Department of Political Science, University of Amsterdam, Amsterdam, Netherlands Andreas Duit Department of Political Science, Stockholm University, Stockholm, Sweden Abstract Building on the burgeoning literature on the association between the welfare state and the environmental state, this study empirically examines how the politics of the former has affected the development of the latter. We suggest that the size of the welfare state shapes the calculus of environmental policy costs by partisan governments. A generous welfare state lowers the costs perceived by the left- wing government, as large redistributive spending allows the government to mitigate the adverse impact of the new environmental policy on its core supporters, industrial workers. A generous welfare state also implies diminished marginal political returns from additional welfare commitment by the left-wing government, which lowers the opportunity costs of environmental policy expansion. To the contrary, because of lower overall regulatory and taxation pressure, a small welfare state reduces the costs of environmental policy expansion as perceived by a right-wing government. Our theoretical narrative is supported in a dynamic panel data analysis of environmental policy outputs in 25 Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development member states during the period 1975–2005. Keywords: environmental regulation, government partisanship, welfare state. 1. Introduction Since the late 1960s, states have set up comprehensive administrative structures and implemented a broad range of pol- icies for the purpose of combatting environmental problems. This expansion of state responsibility in the environmen- tal area has led some scholars to suggest the emergence of an environmental state (Barry & Eckersley 2005; Meadowcroft 2005; Duit 2014). The environmental state is often viewed as analogous to the welfare state in that both use regulatory powers to address market externalities (Christoff 2005; Jahn 2014). Just as welfare state expansion entails increasing governmental intervention in the market for the purpose of providing social goods, environmental policy expansion essentially means increasing market intervention by governments in providing otherwise under-provided environmental goods. The analogy between the welfare state and the environmental state has encouraged scholars in comparative envi- ronmental politics to utilize the analytical perspectives advanced in the welfare state literature (Gough & Meadowcroft 2011; Meadowcroft 2012). A long-standing focus of the welfare state literature on class-based distributive conﬂicts and institutional conﬁgurations of political representation has inﬂuenced researchers in comparative environmental poli- tics. A fair number of empirical studies have examined how government partisanship, corporatist institutions, and elec- toral systems affect a country’s environmental performance (Fiorino 2011; Cao et al. 2014). More recently, studies have also examined how the size of the welfare state is associated with national environmental performance (Jahn 2014; Koch & Fritz 2014). As we discuss in Section II, however, the ﬁndings of these studies are largely inconclusive. In this paper, we seek to move beyond the analogy between the welfare state and the environmental state, and instead explore the dynamic interaction between the two. For this purpose, we develop a theoretical framework spec- ifying how partisan politics in welfare states translate demands for environmental protection into regulatory outputs. While governments face an array of environmental policy demands from domestic and international sources, the electoral rewards from such policies are conﬁned to relatively small sec- tions of the electorate or to diffused legitimation gains (i.e. having a green record). Our focus is then placed on exam- ining how the largely bipartisan environmental policy demands are ﬁltered through a decisionmaking process still structured heavily by traditional partisan politics. We suggest that when confronted with demands for environmental protection, ofﬁce-seeking governments across the left–right ideological spectrum strive for minimizing policy costs as- sociated with adopting a new environmental policy. There are two types of costs involved. First, there are direct costs of the environmental policy on core constituencies, for example, workers’ job losses for leftist governments and increased production costs or administrative burdens for businesses on the part of rightist governments. Second, there are oppor- tunity costs. Pursuing environmental policy expansion requires political as well as budgetary resources that could have been used for the government’s primary policy platforms: redistributive policies for leftist governments and pro- business policies for rightist governments. The lower these two types of costs are, the more likely governments are to adopt a new environmental policy. This is where the welfare state comes into our theoretical narrative. The size of the welfare state affects the cost cal- culus of environmental policy adoption by partisan governments. More speciﬁcally, a generous welfare state reduces the costs of environmental policy expansion perceived by a left-wing government. A generous welfare state not only mit- igates the direct costs of environmental policies accrued to workers, but also implies that the new environmental policy’s opportunity cost (i.e. not being able to engage further in redistributive policies) is low. On the contrary, a small welfare state reduces the costs of environmental policy expansion for a right-wing government. A small welfare state offers businesses greater ﬂexibility to maneuver when a new environmental regulation puts upward pressure on pro- duction costs. A small welfare state also implies that the opportunity costs of the new environmental policy (i.e. not being able to expand pro-business policies) are low. In essence, a left (right)-wing government is more likely to expand environmental policy in times when the welfare spending is relatively large (small). Our dynamic panel data analysis of environmental policy outputs in 25 Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) member states over a 30-year period (1975–2005) lends support to our theoretical framework. 2. Literature and contribution In this section, we review existing studies on government environmental performance that focus on our two key variables of interest: government partisanship and the welfare state. We then discuss how this paper contributes to the existing literature both theoretically and methodologically. 2.1. Does partisan politics explain government environmental performance? A signiﬁcant number of studies have explored whether traditional partisan politics can explain government perfor- mance in protecting the environment. Taking an approach equivalent to class-analytical accounts in the welfare state literature (Korpi 1980, 1989; Huber et al. 1993), some suggest that a left-wing government is more pro-environmental than a right-wing government as the former identiﬁes more with the victims of environmental problems. For instance, the urban poor, who often identify themselves with the left party, are more likely to be victims of industrial pollution and are therefore also more likely to beneﬁt from the expansion of urban environmental protection. Accordingly, suc- cessful political mobilization of environmental demands has been part of a wider left-wing agenda (Pepper 1984). Right-wing parties, to the contrary, are often associated with a less ambitious green agenda because environmental pol- icies impose costs on businesses (Dunlap & Gale 1974; Dunlap & Allen 1976). This, however, might well be an oversimpliﬁcation. The traditionally more organized supporters of the left party, labor unions, have reasons to oppose certain measures for environmental protection.1 Already in the early 1980s, increased environmental regulations in the United States’ fossil-fueled electric power industry were ﬁnd to have led to signiﬁcant job loss in the sector (Gollop & Roberts 1983). Some suggest that “Unionized blue-collar workforces, particularly those in manufacturing and extractive industries” are more likely to face “job loss and economic uncertainty if environmental policy threatens the proﬁtability of their industry” (Obach 2002, p. 83). Duit Welfare States & Environmental Politics 2 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 3: What is perhaps more important in the context of electoral politics than the actual impact of an environmental policy on jobs, is the perceived impact on core constituents. The so-called “jobs versus the environment” sentiment may shape the negative attitudes of left party voters toward environmental regulation, even when the overall/average relationship between environmental regulation and jobs is positive (Morgenstern et al. 2002). Isolated yet salient industry or ﬁrm-level experience (e.g. the fossil-fueled electric power industry) can drive such negative attitudes. As early as 1990, a poll conducted by the Wall Street Journal ﬁnds that one-third of the respondents thought it “somewhat or very likely that their own job was threatened by environmental regulation” (Rosewicz 1990). Also, if the job creation effects of environmental regulations are more concentrated on some industries (manufacturing and professional, information, scientiﬁc, and technical services according to Bezdek et al. 2008), other industries that do not beneﬁt as much may sustain the “jobs versus the environment” attitude. Indeed, recent studies ﬁnd that there is a signiﬁcant sectoral or industry-wise variation in environmental demands among the supporters of the left party. For example, a recent survey analysis conducted in Sweden shows that unions in production industries tend to take a much more reluctant stance toward progressive climate policies than unions in other sectors (Gingrich 2013). Likewise, the traditionally more organized supporters of the right party, businesses, might not necessarily oppose stringent environmental regulations. Certain environmental policies may have little undermining effect on business proﬁtability or even enhance production efﬁciency. A case in point is the US waste processing industry that adopted a labor-saving, automatized recycling system to the detriment of employees (Weinberg et al. 2000). Businesses might also demand stringent environmental policies requiring speciﬁc technologies that allow local businesses to enjoy the ﬁrst mover, or the top runner, advantage (Vogel 1997). Japan had a strong “eco-modernizing coalition” during the 1970s thanks to the “environmental optimism within parts of the industry, and in ﬁelds such as renewable energy and energy efﬁciency,” which encouraged its right-wing government to adopt the hard-core pioneer role in environ- mental policy amounting to “green protectionism” (Jänicke 2005, pp. 132, 136). More recently, the right-wing govern- ment in British Columbia, Canada surprised many observers by adopting a comprehensive carbon tax scheme (Harrison 2010). Essential in this process was the absence of any backlash from businesses. Business communities were indeed willing to grant a greater leeway to the government led by the self-proclaimed “free enterprise” Liberal Party than it would have been to the left-wing government led by the social-democratic New Democratic Party (Harrison 2013). Findings from a few large-N empirical studies mirror this Janus-faced relationship between business, labor, and en- vironmental regulation. Some ﬁnd a positive association between left-libertarian party strength and national environ- mental performance measured using the pollution index (Jahn 1998). Others understand this relationship to be more nuanced. Neumayer (2003), for instance, ﬁnds that a strong presence of green/libertarian left-wing parties is associated with lower emissions of major air pollutants, but he does not ﬁnd the effect of traditional left-wing parties to be consistent over different models in his study. Still others do not ﬁnd any signiﬁcant association between the traditional left–right partisanship and environmental performance measured by policy outputs (Knill et al. 2010). 2.2. Are generous welfare states more committed to environmental protection? What about the effect of the welfare state on environmental performance? If the rationale for expecting diverging environmental policy preferences has in part to do with the preferred “scope of government activities, especially in an effort to solve perceived societal problems” (Dunlap & Allen 1976, p. 395), one should expect a large welfare state to be more pro-environmental overall than a small welfare state. After all, both environmental and welfare policies are about using state powers to address market externalities (Christoff 2005; Jahn 2014; Koch & Fritz 2014). Experience with the interventionist welfare institutions might allow citizens to be more favorable to the interventionist environ- mental policy, regardless of their partisan allegiances. In this vein, scholars have argued that (neo)corporatist institutions in governing labor-business relations have a positive effect on environmental protection (Jahn 1998; Scruggs 1999, 2001). The institutional scope and centralization arguably allow both labor and employers to better consider the negative environmental externalities of their decisions and pursue joint solutions, thus diffusing “best practices” more readily throughout the economy. Yet some small-N case studies of corporatist countries ﬁnd that corporatism does not always facilitate environmental policymaking. Mol’s (2003, p. 342) comparative study of four European countries suggests that the traditional corporatist institutions “do not succeed in maintaining their dominant role” in joint environmental policy arrangements. The study furthermore suggests that the effect of corporatist institutions may vary over time and/or by policy types. Welfare States & Environmental Politics S. Lim and A. Duit © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 Page 4: Some recent studies have moved on to examine the direct effect of the welfare state on national environmental per- formances. Kerret and Shvartzvald’s (2012) cross-country study ﬁnds a positive association between the social policy index (SPI) and the environmental performance index (EPI). Bernauer and Böhmelt’s (2013) cross-sectional study, however, ﬁnds no robust evidence for the claim that countries with better social policies are also likely to be greener, when outliers are removed and the EPI index decomposed. Koch and Fritz (2014) investigate whether country clusters along welfare state typologies (i.e. liberal, conservative, and social democratic welfare states) correspond with country clusters based on sustainability indicators or what they call “eco-state clusters.” They ﬁnd little evidence for correspon- dence. Instead, countries from all welfare regimes are “spread across established, deadlocked, failing, emerging and en- dangered eco-states” (Koch & Fritz 2014, p. 679). 2.3. Why are existing studies inconclusive? Why do we encounter such inconclusiveness in the existing literature? One quick answer may be a lack of consistency between theoretical conceptualization and empirical operationalization of environmental performance. The vast majority of the existing empirical literature focuses on environmental outcomes, such as selected emission statistics or a composite index of multiple outcome indicators, such as the EPI (Emerson et al. 2012). This is the case even when the causal story being put to test focuses explicitly on the scope of governmental engagement with environmental problems, or, in other words, policy outputs. Relying on outcome indicators inevitably leads to black-boxing the better part of the environmen- tal policy dynamic, which, in turn, precludes the understanding of how environmental problems are transformed into environmental policy responses (Meadowcroft 2014). Environmental policy output data so far have been limited in both geographical and temporal coverage (see, however, Knill et al. 2010, 2011 for exceptions). As a partial remedy to this problem, this paper utilizes a newly extended dataset on national environmental policy outputs that encompasses 25 broad environmental policy areas. Our empirical focus on policy output is consistent with our theoretical focus on the government as the main actor who decides whether to engage in environmental policy expansion or not. A second and related limitation of previous work is that most rely on static, cross-sectional comparisons. We suggest that environmental policy expansion should be viewed as a process that unfolds over long time periods, and that the em- pirical analysis should take the nature of the process into account. As was the case in the welfare state development, the process of the environmental state development is expected to entail alternating periods of innovation, relative stagnation, and consolidation (Meadowcroft 2005). The effects partisan politics and the welfare state have on environmental policy expansion might not be immediate but instead distributed over future time periods. Our dynamic panel data analysis covering the period 1975–2005 enables us to move beyond the limitations of snapshot between-country comparisons. Finally, existing studies tend to draw on the analytical parallels between welfare and environmental policies. Only recently have some conceptual works on the environmental state started to acknowledge the difference between the two policy areas and the potential interaction between them. For instance, Gough and Meadowcroft (2011) note that in most advanced democracies, welfare policies have traditionally “trumped” environmental policies. The reasons are twofold. First, the socioeconomic risks that the welfare policies seek to address “bite harder and/or more rapidly than do indirect effects of environmental deterioration” (Gough & Meadowcroft 2011, p. 495) Second, welfare systems have over time “nurtured interest coalitions” within the traditional political scene. Predicting a government’s move toward or against environmental regulation thus requires one to understand ﬁrst the government’s commitment to respond to socioeconomic risks, and then to assess how the new environmental policy may affect such commitment. 3. Theoretical framework The main actor in our theoretical framework is a representative government that seeks political survival. We assume that ruling political parties (coalitions) across the left–right ideological spectrum have distinct policy commitments in areas where they can strongly appeal to their core constituencies. These are usually policy areas that involve tangible (re)distribution of resources. A government led by a left-wing party/coalition would want to expand redistributive spending and maintain it at a high level to offer distributive beneﬁts to workers, whereas a government led by a right-wing party/coalition would want to reduce such spending or maintain it at a low level to protect business interests. Such commitments are predominantly, although not exclusively, reﬂected in social policy. Across countries and over time, left-wing governments have sought to increase the size of the welfare state, while right-wing governments have sought its retrenchment (Korpi 1980, 1989; Huber et al. 1993; Garrett 1998; Allan & Scruggs 2004). Duit Welfare States & Environmental Politics 4 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 5: Unlike social policy, environmental policy squares poorly with traditional partisan politics. While environmental issues are gaining salience among citizens in most industrialized countries (Dunlap & York 2012), studies have shown that core supporters of environmental policy expansion are “clearly different both from manual workers as well as from the old middle class, the two antagonists of the traditional economic division” (Dolezal 2010, p. 537). As highly edu- cated, young, libertarian urban voters tend to lend support for Green parties, the mainstream parties across the political spectrum have been incentivized to form electoral alliance with the Green parties or to incorporate environmental agendas in their own platforms (Dryzek et al. 2002; List & Sturm 2004; O’Neill 2012). Organized domestic environmen- tal lobby groups actively, and sometimes successfully, call for greater government intervention in environmental pro- tection (Fredriksson et al. 2005). Some suggest that environmental policy has gradually become one of the key “valence” issues in electoral politics as a result of the increasing problem pressures and issue awareness (Rohrschneider 1988; Hofrichter & Reif 1990; Green 2007). There have also been international pressures for environmental policy expansion. Upward regulatory competition for sustaining or catching up with lead markets in green technology is found to be an important driver of national environ- mental policy expansion in environmental pioneer countries (Vogel 1997; Jänicke 2005). For the newly industrialized countries in Eastern Europe, Latin America and Asia, joining the OECD club of rich countries or the European Union (EU) has been an important driver of environmental policy expansion (Knill et al. 2008; Sommerer & Lim 2016). The remarkable environmental policy expansion observed in the OECD economies over the last three decades can be viewed as the joint effect of all of these demands (Fiorino 2011; Weidner & Jänicke 2002; Duit 2014). So what role does traditional partisan politics play in this process? We suggest that even when there are strong demands for environmental regulations that parties across the ideological spectrum cannot ignore, the process of translating such demands into a concrete policy output still involves partisan politics to its core. This is because governments across the left–right ideological spectrum have distinct and persistent commitments to their primary policy platforms that appeal to their core supporters. Ofﬁce-seeking governments are unwilling to compromise or jeopardize these platforms for the sake of producing environmental regulations. In essence, at a given level of environmental demand, environmental policy expansion is costlier and therefore less likely when it is perceived to undermine the government’s primary policy platforms. This is how welfare state politics comes into our theoretical narrative. The size of the existing welfare state affects the cost calculus of a partisan government faced with environmental demands. More speciﬁcally, the welfare state shapes the calculus of two distinct types of costs. First, there are direct costs of the environmental policy accrued to the government’s core supporters, for example, workers’ job losses for a left-wing government and increased production costs or adminis- trative burdens for businesses on the part of a right-wing government. These costs directly undermine the government’s distributive commitments. In any given country, a left-wing government would evaluate whether a new environmental policy may have a negative (or perceivably negative) effect on wage and employment prospects. A left-wing government is more likely to engage in environmental policy expansion when such (perceived) risks on workers well-being can be contained or when it can convince workers so. Because redistributive social spending can mitigate the environmental policy’s adverse effect on workers, such as job losses, a left-wing government is more likely to expand environmental policy under a generous welfare state. On the other hand, a right-wing government would fear that a new environmental policy might affect production costs and proﬁt margin of businesses. It is more likely to engage in environmental policy expansion when such costs on businesses are expected to be low or when it can convince businesses that the costs can be contained. Thus, the right-wing government is more likely to adopt a new environmental policy under a small welfare state, which gives more ﬂexibility for the businesses to recover regulatory costs. Second, there are opportunity costs. Environmental policy expansion involves spending political resources that could have been used for the government’s primary policy platforms directed at core constituencies, for example, re- distributive social policies for a left-wing government and pro-business policies for a right-wing government. When the opportunity costs are high, the government is unlikely to engage in environmental policy expansion. Yet as the mar- ginal returns from primary policy platforms decrease, the opportunity costs involved in environmental policy expansion also decrease. This may spur a government to pass environmental policies in order to expand its support base into sections of the electorate for which green policies are a primary policy preference, and to strengthen their overall green image in the eyes of the electorate as a whole. In any given country, a left-wing government would be more willing to adopt a new environmental policy under a generous welfare state. This is because when there is already a generous welfare state, the marginal returns to the government of further expanding redistributive social spending is small. To the contrary, a right-wing government would be more willing to pursue environmental policy expansion under a small Welfare States & Environmental Politics S. Lim and A. Our main hypothesis is as follows: Hypothesis 1: The effect of government partisanship on environmental policy expansion is conditioned by the size of the welfare state. More speciﬁcally, Hypothesis 1.1: A generous welfare state facilitates environmental policy expansion by a left-wing government. Hypothesis 1.2: A small welfare state facilitates environmental policy expansion by a right-wing government. Hypothesis 1 can be pitted against two alternative hypotheses suggested by the existing studies we surveyed earlier. One draws from the literature on the partisan divide in environmental politics, especially in the context of American politics (Dunlap & Gale 1974; Dunlap & Allen 1976; Guber 2001, 2013): Hypothesis 2: A left-wing government is associated with greater environmental policy expansion than a right-wing government (regardless of the size of the welfare state). Empirical Analysis Our hypotheses are tested in dynamic panel data analysis of 25 environmental policy outputs in an unbalanced panel of 25 OECD countries during the period 1975–2005.2 Our sample includes: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Ireland, Italy, Japan, Mexico, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Poland, Portugal, South Korea, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the United Kingdom and the US.3 4.1. Dependent variable Our dependent variable is environmental policy output. We collected data on the enactment of 25 different environ- mental policies, which include a broad range of environmental issues and policy instruments (see Table 1).4 Ofﬁcial governmental records and documents, legal collections, and secondary literature were examined to locate policies. Only those policies issued by the executive or parliament were included in the dataset, and each of the 25 policies was deﬁned by a set of criteria that must be fulﬁlled for it to be counted as a policy. As the total number of environmental policies in a given country is unknown, it was not possible to select policies based on random sampling. We nevertheless believe that these policies are representative of a larger policy universe and that the policy expansion in these areas reﬂects governments’ overall environmental policy commitment. As our main dependent variable, we create the environmental policy scope. It is a proportional variable rang- ing between 0 and 1, and takes the value of 1 when a country-year observation has all 25 sample policies in place. While the actual cost of a speciﬁc policy is determined by the economic, physical, and political context in which the policy is designed and implemented, our variable is based on the simple assumption that more policies take more commitments than fewer policies. Following a conventional technique, we logit-transform the proportional variable (Aitchison 1986).5 The transformation addresses the bounded nature of the observed outcome and allows the effect of an independent variable on a proportional dependent variable to be non- linear.6 For robustness checks, we employ two alternative operationalizations of the dependent variable: raw number of policies in place (ranging from 0 to 25), and adoption of a policy as a binary outcome variable (0 when the policy is absent in a given area, and 1 when the policy is adopted).7 As an additional robustness check, we employ an alternative indicator of environmental policy output, environmental tax per capita (in US$100), to see if our framework can also explain the depth of a single environmental policy, as opposed to the mere introduction of a policy. 4.2. Key independent variables Our key independent variables are government partisanship (left vs. right) and the size of the welfare state. For govern- ment partisanship, we use a dummy variable. Government partisanship equals 1 where there is a left-wing governing party (/coalition) and 0 where there is a right-wing governing party (/coalition).8 For a small number of observations (less than ﬁve percent) governed by center parties, we assign the value of 1 as center parties have more often aligned with left-wing than right-wing parties in the countries in our sample. Our results do not change when we drop the observations that are governed by center parties. Importantly, we do not use vote/seat share operationalized in a per- centage scale because our theoretical framework centers on partisan dominance in government. While the actual threshold vote/seat share for enjoying a dominant position may vary across countries, a percentage increase in right (left) parties’ vote/seat share is unlikely to have any substantive effect on environmental policy outputs without reaching that threshold. For the size of the welfare state, we use the budgetary social protection outlay as a percentage of total central gov- ernment outlays. The social protection outlay includes spending on various social insurance programs, means-tested and in-kind social assistance programs, as well as research and development on all of these programs. The measure Welfare States & Environmental Politics S. Lim and A. Controls We include a set of variables to control for the variation in external and domestic sources of environmental policy demands. First, we control for three core indicators of economic modernization: gross domestic product per capita (in constant 2007 US dollars and adjusted for purchasing power parity), deindustrialization, and urbaniza- tion.10 These indicators have been argued to have a positive association with environmental protection (Mol et al. 2009; O’Neill 2012). Second, we control for trade dependency, EU membership (the year when the accession pro- cess begins), and environmental treaties membership, all of which capture externally originated pressures on en- vironmental quality (Holzinger et al. 2008; Vogel 1997).11 Another potential driving force of environmental policy expansion is environmental administration, which most countries began to set up in the 1960s. We control for the presence of the ministry of the environment and/or an environmental protection agency (Weidner & Jänicke 2002). In addition to the basic set of control variables, we introduce four additional control variables. As adding these variables substantially reduces the sample size, we introduce them in selected models only. First, we add an indicator of political-institutional constraints that might independently affect the ease and incentives for producing environ- mental public goods. More speciﬁcally, we control for the time-varying veto points using Henisz’s (2006) Political Constraint Index Dataset (POLCON) III. We further weigh the veto points with the electoral system (propor- tional = 1, majoritarian = 2), as it has been suggested that the majoritarian system entails stiffer competition than the proportional system, as elected politicians are more direly and separately accountable to the voters (Persson & Tabellini 1999). In a majoritarian system, politicians end up competing more ﬁercely for transfers while under- providing public goods (such as environmental protection) that beneﬁt a broader segment of the population (Fredriksson et al. 2005; Ward & Cao 2012). Thus, our intuition is that institutional constraints would be highest when a government elected by a majoritarian electoral system also faces many time-varying veto points (e.g. by hav- ing a divided government). We expect the electoral system-weighted veto points to have a negative association with the environmental policy scope. Second, we add the unemployment rate to capture potential shocks on the labor market. This variable is important in two regards. First, the rate of unemployment as a proxy for economic cycles may directly affect government’s policy prioritization.12 Second, and perhaps more importantly for testing our hypothesis, a short-term ﬂuctuation in labor market conditions (e.g. as a result of business cycles) might result in ﬂuctuations in social spending that have little to do with the partisan welfare state commitment (e.g. as a result of a sudden increase in unemployment beneﬁts applications). Controlling for the unemployment rate thus allows us to tease out the social spending that reﬂects the longer-term welfare state commitment from that which reﬂects short-term ﬂuctuations. We also control for environmental issue saliency among political parties. Increased environmental issue salience creates new opportunities for environmental movements and Green parties. It also encourages traditional parties to develop positions on environmental issues, and at times, to enter into strategic alliances with the Greens. To capture the growing salience of the environmental issue, we calculate the vote-weighed average share of environmental manifestos of all parties that competed in the latest preceding election.13 The weighing scheme means that our measure of environmental issue salience is correlated with a strong presence of the green party. We prefer the weighted environmental manifesto variable to other indicators speciﬁc to the presence of green parties in parliament because the former can capture the over time variation in environmental issue salience, even in countries where there have been no green parties in parliament. We expect the variable to have a positive association with environmental policy scope. Finally, we control for the level of corporatism by employing a time-varying corporatism index recently made avail- able (Jahn 2016). This is to capture how “corporatism in ﬂux” may affect environmental policy making (Mol 2003, p. 342). The index operationalizes three conceptual aspects of corporatism (organizational-structural, functional, and scope) using the database on Institutional Characteristics of Trade Unions, Wage Setting, State Intervention and Social Pacts (ICTWSS). The richness of the index allows us to control for changes over time in corporatist arrangements within a country, which might independently shape environmental policy output. Extending the insights from the S. Lim and A. Duit Welfare States & Environmental Politics 8 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 9: existing large-N studies on the corporatism and environmental performance nexus, we expect the corporatism index to have a positive association with environmental policy scope (Jahn 1998; Scruggs 1999, 2001). 4.4. Model We model our main dependent variable, environmental policy scope, using the error correction model (ECM). We chose the ECM over other forms of nested/restricted dynamic speciﬁcations for two reasons. The ﬁrst is to avoid imposing potentially invalid restrictions on the functional form of the lag structure.14 Another important advantage of the ECM is that “variables are parameterized in terms of changes, helping to avoid spurious ﬁndings if the stationarity of the series is in question” (De Boef & Keele 2008, p. 195).
