Consistent with the literature, we also include country ﬁxed effects: αi, to control for country-speciﬁc factors that may inﬂuence a country’s propensity to adopt environmental policies. The inclusion of country ﬁxed effects can pick up the effects of largely time-invariant political institutions, such as federalism, electoral rules, and legislative organiza- tion (e.g. bicameralism) (Fredriksson & Millimet 2004, 2007), term limits for elected ofﬁcials (Fredriksson et al. 2011), and ministerial discretion (Bäck et al. 2015). In a dynamic model, the effect of an independent variable can be examined in two aspects: the instantaneous (or ephemeral) effect and the long-run effect. The changes in the independent variable in some previous time points affect the dependent variable, yet the effect is distributed across several future time periods. For instance, in our case, the level of social spending may affect environmental policy outputs both contemporaneously and into the future. As a result of the nature of policymaking, which often takes several years from proposal to legislation, we expect the theorized effect of partisan politics of the welfare state would be more pronounced in the long run. The ECM speciﬁcation estimates the instantaneous effect directly, which is captured by β1. To identify the long-run effect an independent variable has on the dependent variable distributed over future time periods, we calculate and report each independent variable’s long-run multiplier (LRM) as follows:15 LRMx ¼ β2x β0 (2) The delta method is used to calculate the correct standard errors for the LRM effect. The standard errors are ad- justed using Driscoll and Kraay’s (1998) robust variance–covariance estimator, which is a non-parametric technique designed for panel data estimated with country ﬁxed effects. The estimator addresses both group-wise contemporane- ous correlation and heteroskedasticity. 4.5. Findings The results of our empirical analysis are presented in Table 2. We ﬁrst ﬁt a null model, where we look at the effect of government partisanship and welfare spending without including the interaction term of the two (Model 1). Both in the short-term and the long run, we ﬁnd that government partisanship has no signiﬁcant impact on environmental policy expansion. This is against Hypothesis 2, which posited that a left-wing government is associated with greater environmental policy expansion than a right-wing government. Our ﬁnding in the null model is consistent with our theoretical underpinning that there is no strong partisan divergence over environmental protection per se. In the null model, we also ﬁnd that size of social spending has a negative association with environmental policy scope. This ﬁnding suggests that rising welfare spending tends to increase the governments’ perceived costs of a new environmental policy. This weakens Hypothesis 3, which posited that a generous welfare state facilitates environmental policy expansion (regardless of government partisanship). In Model 2, we explore how government partisanship and the size of the welfare state interactively affect environmental policy scope. In Models 3–5 we introduce additional control variables: Model 3 adds unemploy- ment rate and political constraint, Model 4 controls in addition for environmental issue salience, and Model 5 further controls for corporatist arrangements. We ﬁnd a statistically meaningful long-run interaction effect across all interaction models. In light of our main hypotheses (i.e. Hypotheses 1.1 and 1.2 concerning the conditioning role of the size of the welfare state), we interpret the interaction term focusing on the long-run marginal effect of S. Lim and A. Duit Welfare States & Environmental Politics 10 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 11: government partisanship. Following Brambor et al. (2006), we graphically display the long-run marginal effect of left party dominance in government on environmental policy scope at various levels of welfare spending in Figure 1 (based on Model 5). Any particular point on the interior solid sloping line indicates a long-run marginal effect of left party dominance at different levels of welfare spending.16 The dotted lines are 90 percent conﬁdence intervals. The long-run marginal effect of left party dominance is negative when the welfare spending is below about 35 percent of the budget, suggesting that a left-wing government has an undermining effect on environmental policy expansion when the welfare state is small. Environmental policy scope would have been wider under right party dominance. However, the marginal effect of the left party dominance becomes increasingly positive when the welfare budget is above 35 percent of total government outlays. This is when a left party coming into power would facilitate environmental policy expansion. Such an interaction effect is observable across Models 2–5, although the slope of the line and conﬁdence intervals vary slightly. This ﬁnding supports Hypotheses 1.1 and 1.2. The same interaction terms can also be interpreted focusing on the long-run marginal effect of welfare spending, while ﬁxing the government partisanship variable at either the left or the right. In Model 2, for example, we ﬁnd that an increase in welfare spending undermines environmental policy expansion under both types of partisan dominance, although the undermining effect is stronger under a right-wing government (0.062) than under a left-wing govern- ment (0.062 + 0.028 = 0.034). In models with additional control variables, the negative effect of increased welfare spending is almost nulliﬁed for the left-wing government (0.006 in Models 3–4 and 0.004 in Model 5). The ﬁnding reafﬁrms our theoretical narrative that a left-wing government sees less tension between the welfare state and the environmental state than a right-wing government. In any case, we do not ﬁnd support for Hypothesis 3, which pos- tulated that a generous welfare state would facilitate environmental policy expansion, regardless of government partisanship. 4.6. Models 6 and 7 tackle concerns about potential casual heterogeneity that might complicate the testing of our theoretical framework. Our theoretical framework is developed in the context of representative democracies in which politicians seek ofﬁce and are thus responsive to the concerns of their core constituents. While the 25 OECD member countries do represent contemporary representative democracies, several non-Western member countries that are relatively new and less mature democracies are included: Hungary, Mexico, Poland, and South Korea.
